program: Travel English -- Europe Without a Translator
hub_subtitle: Travel English for the airport, the check-in counter, the hotel and passport control — so that Europe needs no translator

lesson.menu_title: Have You Just Arrived? -- The First Hour in the Hotel
lesson.menu_desc: Present perfect for what just happened: just, already, yet. Almost midnight in a Lisbon hotel, a long flight behind her, a receptionist asking what she has already done and what she hasn't done yet -- and, for once, nobody to answer for her -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 13 -- Have You Just Arrived?
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Rosângela | Lesson 13 -- Have You Just Arrived?
lesson.grammar_point: present perfect with just / already / yet (recent completed actions)

listenings.text: Good evening, everybody, and welcome to the hotel. My name is Henrique, I am the night manager, and it is almost midnight, so I am going to be quick, because you have all had a very long day. Look at your faces. Some of you have just landed. Some of you have been on a plane for eleven hours. And some of you, I can see, have already fallen asleep standing up, and that is fine. So, three things, and then your keys. First: your rooms are ready. All of them. We have already cleaned them, we have already put out the water and the towels, and the beds are turned down. You do not have to do anything tonight. Drop off your bags, and go to sleep. Second: breakfast. It is downstairs, and it starts at seven and it finishes at ten. Now, listen to me. If you have just arrived from Brazil, or from Japan, or from anywhere far, you have not slept in your own bed for a day, and your body does not know what time it is. So do not put an alarm for six. Sleep. If you miss breakfast, we will find you a coffee. Nobody has ever died of a late breakfast in this hotel. Third, and this is the one people forget. If you want a wake-up call, call reception now, tonight, before you go up. Do not wait until the morning. Half of the people who miss their tour have not asked for the wake-up call yet. They think they will remember, and then jet lag wins. It always wins. So, have you asked for your wake-up call? No? The phone is right here. Now, your keys. And welcome to Lisbon. You have already done the hard part. You have arrived.

Hello. My name is Margarida, I am seventy-two, and I want to tell you about the first hour in a hotel, alone, because I think nobody tells you about that hour, and it is the hardest one. Not the airport. Not the plane. The first hour in the room. Here is what happens. You have just arrived. You are exhausted, you are jet-lagged, it is late, and you open the door of a room you have never seen, and it is silent, and it is not your home. And for about one minute, every single time, I want to go back. Seventy-two years old, nineteen countries, and I still want to go home for one minute in that doorway. So this is what I have learned to do, and I am going to give it to you, because it works. Do not sit down. If you sit down on that bed, jet-lagged, at midnight, it is over. Instead, do three small things, in the same order, every time. One: I drop off my bag and I open the curtains, even at night, so I can see where I am in the morning. Two: I unpack one thing. Not everything. One thing. Usually a photograph. And all of a sudden the room is a little bit mine. Three: I freshen up, I wash my face with cold water, I look in the mirror, and I say, out loud, in English, so it counts: I have arrived. I made it. And that is the hour. After that the room is yours, and the trip begins. I have done this in nineteen countries. My husband is not with me any more. He was the one who used to do the talking, and now it is me, and I am telling you: the language was never the hard part. The doorway was the hard part. And you have already walked through worse doorways than this one. Go. Open the curtains.

structure.title: Three little words that change where they stand
cards: a moment ago -- it stands in the MIDDLE
"I have JUST arrived." It happened a very short time ago, almost now. Put it between have and the verb: have + just + past participle. Never "I just have arrived", never "I have arrived just now".
sooner than you thought -- also in the MIDDLE
"I have ALREADY unpacked." It is done, and it is done early -- it is good news, a small surprise. Same place as just: have + already + past participle.
up to now -- and it lives at the END
"I haven't had dinner YET." "Have you checked in YET?" Only in questions and in the negative, and always at the end of the sentence. Yet is the thing that is still waiting to happen.

items.situation: It is almost midnight. The receptionist looks at your face and asks: "Have you just arrived?" Answer, and say how you feel.
tips: Yes, I have just arrived. The flight has just landed, and I am exhausted.
Yes, I have just arrived, and I am so jet-lagged I cannot think.

items.situation: He asks if you have checked in online. You did it this morning, before the airport. Use the good-news word.
tips: Yes, I have already checked in online, this morning.
I have already checked in. I did it on my phone before the flight.

items.situation: He asks if you have had dinner. You have not. Use the word that waits at the end.
tips: No, I haven't had dinner yet. Is the restaurant still open?
Not yet. I haven't eaten anything since the plane.

items.situation: Your bags are already up in the room -- you dropped them off ten minutes ago. Your husband asks about them. Tell him.
tips: I have already dropped off the bags. They are in the room.
Don't worry, I have already taken the bags upstairs.

items.situation: You want a wake-up call for seven o'clock. Ask the receptionist for it, politely.
tips: Could I have a wake-up call at seven, please?
Can you give me a wake-up call at seven tomorrow?

items.situation: Careful now -- he asks WHEN you arrived in Lisbon, the city. You came three days ago. That is a finished time.
tips: I arrived in Lisbon three days ago. (A finished time, so past simple, not "I have arrived three days ago".)
I came three days ago. Today I have just arrived at the hotel, and that is different.

items.situation: It is early morning. You are hungry and you want to know if breakfast is open. Ask, with the end word.
tips: Excuse me, has the restaurant opened yet?

items.situation: He asks if this is your first time in Portugal. It is not -- you came once in 2019. Answer with BOTH tenses.
tips: No, I have been to Portugal before. I came in 2019.
I have been here once. I came in 2019, with my son.
